meta.setting: Marigold, a fictional river town, autumn 1974
meta.protagonist: Opal, in her seventies, notices small things
meta.note: Lines are written to be read slowly. Pauses are part of the meaning.

scene.title: The Lantern Festival
scene.line: The lanterns go up the night before, so the street remembers the morning of.
Opal. You came down. I saved you the corner table and the first batch.
You say that every year, Hazel, and every year it is the second batch.
And every year you eat it anyway. Sit. The festival can wait one biscuit.

choice.option: Watch the street fill up first

scene.line: There. One small light, and the whole evening leans toward it.
No hurry. The town shows you more when you let it arrive.

scene.title: The River Road
scene.line: The bridge keeps two lanterns. One for the going, one for the coming back.
Mind the second one. Folks light the first and forget the road runs both ways.
I have stood at this rail before. The water kept the date I lost.

scene.title: The Letter
scene.line: She calls every Sunday from the city. The line is never long enough.
Keep the letters. The phone forgets. Paper holds what a voice lets go.
